era_context: The Old Kingdom of Egypt (Dynasty VI) is in its final century, with power increasingly decentralized to provincial governors. The Akkadian Empire under Naram-Sin is at its height. The Indus Valley Civilization is in its mature, prosperous phase. In China, the Xia dynasty (if historical) would be in its early period, though archaeological evidence remains contested.

events.title: Pepi II of Egypt — reign in its later phase
events.description: Pepi II's reign continues. The pharaonic state remains functional but increasingly dependent on the cooperation of provincial elites. The construction of royal monuments continues but at a reduced scale compared to earlier Old Kingdom dynasties. Trade expeditions to Nubia and the Levant are documented in the autobiographies of officials such as Harkhuf, suggesting continued commercial activity despite political fragmentation.

sources.name: Autobiographical inscriptions of Harkhuf (tomb at Aswan)

events.certainty_note: The general context is well-attested; specific events in this year are not individually documented.
cross_references: Part of the Old Kingdom decline, -2510 to -2480

events.title: Naram-Sin of Akkad — imperial consolidation and deification
events.description: Naram-Sin's reign continues with the consolidation of Akkadian imperial ideology. He is deified and worshipped as a god, a radical departure from earlier Mesopotamian kingship. This ideological shift is reflected in inscriptions and administrative texts. The Akkadian Empire reaches its maximum territorial extent, controlling much of Mesopotamia, the Levant, and parts of Anatolia.
events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's deification is well-attested in contemporary inscriptions; the specific year is not documented.
cross_references: Part of Akkadian imperial expansion, -2254 to -2218 BCE

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization — mature phase urbanism and trade
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization continues in its mature phase. Major urban centers such as Mohenjo-daro and Harappa function as centers of craft production, trade, and administration. Standardized weights, measures, and seals indicate sophisticated systems of commerce. Trade networks extend to Mesopotamia, the Persian Gulf, and Central Asia.
events.certainty_note: The mature Indus phase is archaeologically well-established; specific events in this year are not documented.
cross_references: Continuation of mature Indus phase, c. -2600 to -1900

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. The chronological placement of this year remains uncertain due to competing chronologies for the Old Kingdom and Akkadian periods.
historiographic_note: This year falls within a period of sparse documentation. The surviving sources are primarily monumental inscriptions and administrative texts, which are selective and propagandistic. Specific events in this year are largely unknown. Chronological uncertainty remains significant.

graph_edges.note: Pepi II's reign continues in parallel with the previous year
